name: gwtools

metadata.name: gwtools
metadata.summary: A collection of gravitational wave tools

documentation:
# README #

A collection of useful functions and tools for working with gravitational waveform data.

# Install #

To install from pypi

```bash
pip install gwtools
```

To install from source

```bash
git clone git@bitbucket.org:chadgalley/gwtools.git
pip install -e gwtools
```

where the "-e" installs an editable (development) project with pip. This allows
your local code edits to be automatically seen by the system-wide installation.

modules.name: gwtools.const
documentation:
This file defines constants used by romgw, gwsurrogate and gwtools.

Many of the older values of these constants are commented out in case 
previous tests/analysis needs to be repeated.

variables.name: gwtools.const.c
documentation: Speed of light (m/s)

variables.name: gwtools.const.Msuninsec
documentation: Solar mass (seconds) = lal.MSUN_SI * lal.G_SI / (lal.C_SI**3.0)

variables.name: gwtools.const.PCinm
documentation: Parsec (meters)

variables.name: gwtools.const.gamma_E
documentation: Euler-Mascheroni constant

variables.name: gwtools.const.G
documentation: Gravitation constant (m^3/kg/s^2)

variables.name: gwtools.const.Msun
documentation: Solar mass (kg)

variables.name: gwtools.const.Mpcinm
documentation: Megaparsec (meters)

variables.name: gwtools.const.Mpcinkm
documentation: Megaparsec (km)

variables.name: gwtools.const.Msuninkg
documentation:
# romgw constants, and older gwsurroate constants # 
# Older version of code used these values
G         = 6.67428e-11      # Gravitation constant (MKS)
Msun      = 1.98892e30       # Solar mass (kg)
Msuninsec = Msun * G / c**3  # Solar mass (secs)
Mpcinm    = 3.08568025e22    # Megaparsecs (m)
Mpcinkm   = Mpcinm/1000.0    # Megaparsecs (km)
Msuninkg  = Msun             # for romgw 

# Jonathan's constants
#Msun = 1.9891e30
#Mpcinm = 3.08567758e22
#SPEED_OF_LIGHT_C = 299792458
#GRAVITATIONAL_CONSTANT_G = 6.67384e-11
#G = 6.67384e-11
#Msuninsec = Msun * G / c**3
#Mpcinkm   = Mpcinm/1000.0    # Megaparsecs (km)
for romgw

modules.name: gwtools.gwutils
documentation: A collection of useful gravitational wave tools
--- gwutils.py

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils._planckWindowFunction
documentation:
f(t) = 0 if t < tol
= 1 if t > 1 - tol
= 1/(1 + exp(z)) otherwise, where z = 1/t - 1/(1-t)
Tapering windows

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils._cosineWindowFunction
documentation:
f(t) = 0 if t < 0
= 1 if t > 1
= (1 - cos(pi*t))/2 otherwise

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils.window
documentation:
Return a smooth rollon function, evaluated at an array of times t:
When t <= tStart, f(t) = 0
When t >= tEnd, f(t) = 1
Otherwise, 0 <= f(t) <= 1, where f is monotonically increasing.

If rolloff=1, reverse the window so that f(tStart) = 1, f(tEnd) = 0.

windowType may be:
    'planck'
    'cosine'

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils.windowWaveform
documentation:
Rolls on from t1 to t2, then rolls off from t3 to t4.

t1-t4 are assumed to have the same units as t.

windowType can be:
    "planck"
    "cosine"

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils.windowWaveformV2
documentation:
Wrapper around windowWaveform. Use when waveform independent variable
units in seconds, while window parameters t1 - t4 are specified
in dimensionless units.

INPUT
=====
i)  nSolarMasses = None

  t1-t4 assumed to have the same units as t.

ii) nSolarMasses = Mtot

  t1-t4 in dimensionless units. t in seconds

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils.freqDomainWaveform
documentation:
A function to fft waveforms, always padding up to a power of 2 and
possibly more.

INPUT
=====
h            --- complex or real values numpy array (should be tapered!)
dt           --- = 1/sampling_rate
dfMax: If the waveform is not long enough, it will be padded with zeros
       until it achieves df <= dfMax.
       If None, only pad up to a power of 2.
padLocation  --- 'start', 'center', 'end', 'random', and
                 determines where the zeros are placed

OUTPUT
=====
frequencies: An array of monotonically increasing frequencies
hf: The frequency domain waveform



EXAMPLE
=======

The following code will show peak at 10 

omega = 10
times = np.linspace(-10,10,1000)
y = np.cos(omega*times) + 1.0j*np.sin(omega*times)
y_tapered = gwutils.windowWaveform(times, y, times[0], times[0] + 100, times[-1] -100, times[-1], windowType='planck')

dt = times[1] - times[0]
freqs_ringdown, hFreq_tapered_y = gwutils.freqDomainWaveform(y_tapered, dt, dfMax=None, padLocation='end')
freqs_ringdown = freqs_ringdown*2*np.pi

plt.semilogy(freqs_ringdown*2*np.pi, np.abs(hFreq_tapered_y),'k--')
Obtaining frequency domain waveforms

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils.freqDomainModes
documentation:
Similar to freqDomainWaveform, but where h is a 2-d array of
complex modes (mode index first).

The returned hf is an array of the fft of each mode.

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils.freqDomainWaveformFromModes
documentation:
Given the fourier transforms of the waveform modes, evaluates the
waveform on the sphere at (theta, phi) with polarization
cos(2*kappa)*h_plus + sin(2*kappa)*h_cross

INPUT
=====
frequencies: The uniformly spaced array of frequencies at which hModes
             is evaluated, symmetrically spaced around 0Hz such that
             frequencies[0] = -1*frequencies[-1].
hModes: A 2-d array of frequency domain waveform modes, mode index first.
        The modes should be ordered [(2, -2), ..., (2, 2), (3, -3), ...]
theta, phi: The spherical polar coordinates at which the waveform
            should be evaluated
kappa: The polarization angle of the waveform.

functions.name: gwtools.gwutils.hp_hc_from_freqDomain_modes
documentation:
Given the fourier transforms of the waveform modes, evaluates the
two waveform polarizations on the sphere at (theta, phi)

INPUT
=====
frequencies: The uniformly spaced array of frequencies at which hModes
             is evaluated, symmetrically spaced around 0Hz such that
             frequencies[0] = -1*frequencies[-1].
hModes: A 2-d array of frequency domain waveform modes, mode index first.
        The modes should be ordered [(2, -2), ..., (2, 2), (3, -3), ...]
theta, phi: The spherical polar coordinates at which the waveform
            should be evaluated

modules.name: gwtools
documentation: import gwtools

modules.name: gwtools.rotations
documentation: A collection of functions related to rotating waveforms with quaternions
--- rotations.py

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.multiplyQuats
documentation: q1, q2 must be [scalar, x, y, z] but those may be arrays or scalars

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.quatInv
documentation: Returns QBar such that Q*QBar = 1

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.minRotQuat_to_phases
documentation:
Given quaternions vs. time for a minimally rotating frame (no instantaneous
rotation in the z-direction), returns (quatPhase, dirPhase) where quatPhase
is the overall precession phase and dirPhase gives the instantaneous
precession direction in the (x, y) plane.
Note that len(dirPhase) == len(quat[0]) - 1

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.phases_to_minRotQuat
documentation: Inverts minRotQuat_to_phases

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.coprecessingQuat
documentation:
Uses GWFrames to find the coprecessing frame of h
Specify iAlign if the quat should be aligned about the z-axis
GWFrames seems to leak memory when accessing quaternion elements:
for i in range(100000):
junk = w.Frame(0)[0]

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.corotatingQuat
documentation: Uses GWFrames to find the corotating frame of h

functions.name: gwtools.rotations._floatFac
documentation: Helper functions for rotating waveform modes

functions.name: gwtools.rotations._floatFacRatio
documentation: Ratio of n! to k! but only computes n*(n-1)*...*(n-k) for speed

functions.name: gwtools.rotations._wignerD
documentation: (Code adapted to python from GWFrames)

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.rotateWaveform
documentation:
Similar to transformWaveform but does not rely on GWFrames.
(Code adapted to python from GWFrames)

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.transformWaveform
documentation:
Transforms a waveform h according to some frame expressed as unit quaternions.
If h is in the coprecessing frame and quat is the coprecessing frame,
using inverse=0 gives the waveform in the inertial frame.
Use inverse=1 if h is in the inertial frame and you want the coprecessing
waveform.

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.omegaOrb_from_waveform
documentation:
Transforms the waveform to the corotating frame and determines an orbital
frequency using the frame rotation rate.

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.alignVec_quat
documentation: Returns a unit quaternion that will align vec with the z-axis

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.zRotationQuat
documentation: Returns a unit quaternion that will rotate about the z-axis

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.transformTimeDependentVector
documentation:
Given (for example) a minimal rotation frame quat, transforms
vec from the inertial frame to the minimal rotation frame.

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.precession_alignment
documentation:
Aligns h using constant-in-time rotations such that at t[iAlign]:
    -The z-direction corresponds to maximal gravitational wave emission
    -The phases of the (2, 2) and (2, -2) modes are equal (and nearly zero)
This leaves an ambiguity of an additional rotation by pi about the z-axis.
It is assumed the waveform is already nearly aligned, in which case we just
choose the smaller rotation.
If nearlyAligned=0, you might have issues.
Typically we need more than just the waveform to resolve this ambiguity,
i.e. we want the larger black hole roughly on the positive x-axis,
and the smaller one on the negative x-axis, with the orbital angular
momentum roughly in the z-direction.

functions.name: gwtools.rotations.filteredCoprQuat
documentation:
Returns a quaternion similar to the minimally rotating
coprecessing quaternion, but with orbital timescale oscillations
filtered out.  It IS minimally rotating (no z component),
but is NOT exactly coprecessing.
widthCycles controls the width of the gaussian filter in number of cycles.
Specify iAlign to ensure the coprecessing frame is aligned at t[iAlign].

modules.name: gwtools.mismatch
documentation:
A collection of useful functions for comparing frequency  and time domain
waveforms and evaluating errors.
Throughout, "overlap_err" is 1 - overlap, NOT optimizing over time shifts,
and "mismatch" is (1 - overlap) minimized over time shifts.
Other parameters are not optimized unless explicitly stated in the docstring.

See docs/mismatches.pdf for mathematics.
--- fitfuncs.py

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.inner_complex
documentation:
Frequency domain weighted complex inner product.

h1, h2 are frequency domain waveforms (complex 1d arrays).
psd can be None for unweighted (equivalent to a psd of ones) or an array
of values the same length as h1 and h2.
Note: PSD = ASD^2, should be ~ 10^-42 (not 10^-21) for aLIGO.


If pos_freq=True, h1 and h2 are interpreted as coming from f>=0 
frequencies and are real function in the time-domain. Then 

  inner = 2 * Real(inner_complex)

which comes from folding f<0 frequencies (accounts for "2 * Real()")


If ligo=True we use the typical gw convention

    inner = 2 * inner_complex


If ligo=True, pos_freq=True we use both of the above rules:

    inner_ligo = 4 * Real(inner_complex)


See, for example,
        https://arxiv.org/pdf/1602.03839.pdf (Eq 1)
        https://arxiv.org/pdf/1602.03509.pdf (Eq 6)
        https://arxiv.org/pdf/gr-qc/0509116.pdf (Eq 4.1)
Inner products

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.inner
documentation:
Real frequency domain inner product.
See inner_complex for details.

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.inner_complex_timeshift_series
documentation:
Uses an inverse fft to compute inner_complex of h1 and
h2*exp(2*pi*i*f*dt) for dt=0, 1/f_max, 2/f_max, ...

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.inner_timeshift_series
documentation: Real part of inner_complex_timeshift_series

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.modes_to_hf
documentation:
Sums spin-weighted spherical harmonic modes (s=-2) evaluated at
(theta, phi) to obtain the waveform.

h_modes: A 2d array with shape (n_modes, n_samples), ordered
    [(2, -2), ..., (2, 2), (3, -3), ..., (3, 3), ...]

returns hf, a 1d array with length n_samples.
h conversions

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch._cyclic_quadratic_peak
documentation:
Finds the peak value of y using a quadratic curve through the max
value and its neighbors. Assumes periodic boundary conditions if the max
is at the edge.
Returns i_peak (float), y_peak.
Utility

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch._timeshift
documentation: Finds the timeshift from inverse fft information

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch._cyclic_interp_phase
documentation:
Uses a cubic spline to interpolate the angle of x onto the
"index" i_interp which can be a float.
Uses periodic boundary conditions.

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.mismatch
documentation:
Minimizes the overlap error over time shifts and (possibly)
polarization angle shifts.
Returns:
    mismatch
    time shift
    polarization angle shift (returns zero if polarization_shift is False)
If df is in Hz, the returned time shift is in seconds.
A quadratic fit to the largest overlap and its nearest neighbors is used
to determine the maximum, but be sure the waveforms are long enough
(pad with zeros if needed) to get a good time-shift resolution!

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.total_amplitude_from_mode_dict
documentation: These functions will compute CalE and related quantities for a dictionary of modes

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.error_vs_time_from_mode_dict
documentation:
Computes the square root of (|\delta h(t)|^2 integrated over the sphere).

Input: dictionary of modes (e.g. {mode1:timeseries_of_mode1,...})
       from a reference (true) waveform, ref_mode, and a second waveform
       model, other_modes
       
Output: time series of waveform error (see mathcal_E_error_from_mode_dict)

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.mathcal_E_error_from_mode_dict
documentation:
Computes Eq 21 from https://arxiv.org/pdf/1701.00550.pdf 

This error is the "natural" time-domain error for multi-mode signals

Input: dictionary of modes (e.g. {mode1:timeseries_of_mode1,...})
       from a reference (true) waveform, ref_mode, and a second waveform
       model, other_modes
       
Output: the error, a single number

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.mathcal_E_mode_contributions_from_mode_dict
documentation:
Mode-by-mode contributions to mathcal E error.

The sum of all these error contributions should equal the 
value from mathcal_E_error_from_mode_dict

Input: dictionary of modes (e.g. {mode1:timeseries_of_mode1,...})
       from a reference (true) waveform, ref_mode, and a second waveform
       model, other_modes
       
Output: the error in each mode, such that the sum is equal to mathcalE error

functions.name: gwtools.mismatch.mathcal_E_error_from_mode_list
documentation:
time-domain error via Eq 21 https://arxiv.org/pdf/1701.00550.pdf 


This error is the "natural" time-domain error for multi-mode signals

Input: list of modes from a reference (true) waveform, h2, and a second waveform
       model, h1. It is assumed that lists h1 and h2 have the same mode ordering.
       
       mode0: h1[0],h2[0]
       mode1: h1[1],h2[1]
       ....

       The function will also accept single modes as a numpy array
       
Output: the error, a single number

modules.name: gwtools.gwtools
documentation: A collection of useful gravitational wave tools
--- gwtools.py ---

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.m1m2_to_Mc
documentation: Chirp mass from m1, m2
Functions for changing parameters #

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.m1m2_to_nu
documentation: Symmetric mass ratio from m1, m2

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.m1m2_to_Mcnu
documentation: Compute symmetric mass ratio and chirp mass from m1, m2

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.q_to_nu
documentation: Convert mass ratio (which is >= 1) to symmetric mass ratio

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.nu_to_q
documentation: Convert symmetric mass ratio to mass ratio (which is >= 1)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.Mq_to_m1m2
documentation: Convert total mass, mass ratio pair to m1, m2

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.Mq_to_Mc
documentation: Convert mass ratio, total mass pair to chirp mass

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.Mcq_to_M
documentation: Convert mass ratio, chirp mass to total mass

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.Mcnu_to_M
documentation: Convert chirp mass and symmetric mass ratio to total mass

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.Mnu_to_Mc
documentation: Convert total mass and symmetric mass ratio to chirp mass

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.Mnu_to_m1m2
documentation: Convert total mass and symmetric mass ratio to m1, m2

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.Mcnu_to_m1m2
documentation: Convert chirp mass, symmetric mass ratio pair to m1, m2

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.m1m2_to_delta
documentation: Convert m1, m2 pair to relative mass difference [delta = (m1-m2)/(m1+m2)]

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.q_to_delta
documentation: Convert mass ratio (which is >= 1) to relative mass difference (delta)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.delta_to_q
documentation: Convert relative mass difference (delta) to mass ratio q (q >= 1)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.delta_to_nu
documentation: Convert relative mass difference (delta) to symmetric mass ratio

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.nu_to_delta
documentation: Convert symmetric mass ratio to relative mass difference delta

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.X1X2_to_Xs
documentation: Convert dimensionless spins X1, X2 to symmetric spin Xs

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.X1X2_to_Xa
documentation: Convert dimensionless spins X1, X2 to anti-symmetric spin Xa

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.X1X2_to_XsXa
documentation: Convert dimensionless spins X1, X2 to symmetric and anti-symmetric spins Xs, Xa

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.Chi1M1Chi2M2_to_chiE
documentation: Convert (chi_i,m_i) pair to chi_effective

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.fgwisco
documentation: GW frequency at ISCO. [Note: Maggiore's text has an extra 1/2.]
Miscellaneous helper functions #

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.get_arg
documentation: Get argument at which a0 occurs in array a

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.get_peak
documentation: Get argument and values of x and y at maximum value of |y|

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.fit_peak
documentation:
Estimate peak of y through interpolation.

Input
=====
x   --- x data array (1d)
y   --- y data array (1d)
fit --- Type of fitting function 
        (default='polyfit')
a   --- Number of points to fit before estimated peak
        (default=10)
b   --- Number of points to fit after estimated peak 
        (default=10)
deg --- Degree or order of fitting function 
        (default=2)
num --- Number of high resolution x samples for estimating peak 
        (default=1000)

Output
======
x_peak --- Estimated x value of the estimated maximum of y data
y_peak --- Estimated maximum of y data

Note: The fitting is currently done with the UnivariateFits class 
in ROMpy. The maximum of the y data is estimated by evaluating the 
fitting function at num(=1000) points. However, the default fitting 
function is a quadratic polynomial, the peak of which is 
calculated analytically instead of numerically.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.chop
documentation: Chop arrays x, y for x in [xmin, xmax]

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.shift
documentation: Shift array x by xshift

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.shift_chop
documentation: Shift x, y arrays by xshift then chop for x in [xmin, xmax]

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.amp
documentation: Get amplitude of waveform, h = A*exp(i*phi)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.amp_phase
documentation:
Get amplitude and phase of waveform, 

h = A*exp(i*phi)       (convention=0)

     or

h = A*exp(-i*phi)      (convention=1)

Convention=1 is more commonly used, but convention=0 is default for
backwards compatibility.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.phase
documentation:
Get phase of waveform,

> h = A\*exp(i\*phi)       (convention=0)
>
> > or
>
> h = A\*exp(-i\*phi)      (convention=1)
>
> Convention=1 is more commonly used, but convention=0 is default for
> backwards compatibility.

Preserves shape ( np.shape(h) == np.shape(phase) ) and gracefully handles h=0

# Input

h          - numpy vector or array of shape (N,1) or (N,)
convention - integer for convention choice above

Examples
\>>> t1 = np.linspace(0,10,100)
\>>> y1 = np.exp(1.0j\*t1)
\>>> phase1 = phase(y1)
\>>> y1.shape
(100,)
\>>> phase1.shape
(100,)
\>>> t3 = np.reshape(t1,(100,1))
\>>> y3 = np.exp(1.0j\*t3)
\>>> phase3 = phase(y3)
\>>> phase3.shape
(100,1)
\>>> y3.shape
(100,1)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.cycles
documentation: Count number of cycles (to merger, if present) in waveform

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.hplus
documentation: Get real part of waveform, h = A*exp(i*phi)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.hcross
documentation: Get imaginary part of waveform, h = A*exp(i*phi)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.logd
documentation: Compute a logarithmic derivative

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.plot_pretty
documentation:
create a waveform figure with nice formatting and labels.
returns figure method for saving, plotting, etc.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.ecc_estimator
documentation:
"Estimate the eccentricity associated with gravitational 
waveform h from Eq. (17) of arxiv:1004.4697 (gr-qc).

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.find_instant_freq
documentation:
instantaneous starting frequency for 

        h = A(t) exp(2 * pi * i * f(t) * t), 

where we approximate \partial_t A ~ \partial_t f ~ 0.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.dimensionless_time
documentation: input time and mass in seconds. Returns dimensionless time

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.dimensionless_frequency
documentation: Input frequency in seconds, Mass in solar masses. Returns dimensionless frequency

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.geo_to_SI
documentation:
Transforms the waveform from geometric unit to physical units.

Input
===== 
t_geo: (numpy array) time in geometric units "T/M". 
h_geo: strain in geometric units "rh/M"

  This should be a dictionary of waveform modes:

     h_geo[(2,2)] is the 22 mode as a numpy array

M_tot: Total mass in solar masses
dist_mpc:  Distance to binary system in MegaParsecs

Output
======
t_SI, h_SI: time and strain in SI units 


Routine author: Tousif Islam

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.remove_amplitude_zero
documentation: removes h[i] t[i] from array if |h[i]| = 0

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.modify_phase
documentation:
Modify GW mode's phase to be \phi(t) -> \phi(t) + offset.
For h_{ell,m}, typically offset = m*z_rot where z_rot is a 
physical rotation about the z-axix (orthogonal to the orbital plane).

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.coordinate_time_shift
documentation: Modify times to be t -> t + offset

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.find_common_time_window
documentation:
Given two temporal grids, find the largest range of common times 
defined by [min_common,max_common]

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.simple_align_params
documentation:
t1 times for complex waveform h1. 

This routine returns simple alignment parameters 
deltaT and deltaPhi by...
  (i)  fining discrete waveform peak
  (ii) aligning phase values at the peak time

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.euclidean_norm_sqrd
documentation: Euclidean norm squared of a complex vector f

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.euclidean_rel_error_sqrd
documentation:
Relative error 

            || f2 - f1||^2 / ( ||f1|| * ||f2|| )

computed from complex vectors f1 and f2

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.euclidean_norm_sqrd_2sphere
documentation:
Euclidean norm squared of a complex function 

h(t,theta,phi) = \sum h_{ell,m}(t) B_{ell,m} (theta,phi) 

known through its harmonic coefficients h_{ell,m}, each
of which are column vector of an input matrix f.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.generate_parameterize_waveform
documentation:
returns function for h(t;tc,phic) evaluations.

INPUT
=====
t         --- array of times
h1_func   --- waveform which can be evaluated for input set of times 
h1_type   --- h1_func type ('interp1d','h_sphere') 
h1_params --- any additional parameters needed for waveform evalautions

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.generate_parameterized_norm
documentation:
this routine will return a parameterized discrete norm 

       N(p1,p2,...) = || h1(p1,p2,...) - h_ref || 

which can be minimized over the parameters (p1,p2...). If p1 = p2 = ... = 0 then 
the norm is simply

               || h1 - h_ref ||   for h1 = h1[t]

h_ref is a reference waveform (represented by a discrete set
evaluations) to which we match another waveform h1 (represented as a
function). 

h1 is a python function which takes inputs p1, p2,...
where, typically, p1 is timeshift (tc) and p2 is a rotation about 
the z-axis phic. The function h1 returns a vector h1(t_c,phi_c) collocated
with h2_ref.


Input
=====
t:         array of times such that h2_ref = h_ref[t]
h2_ref:    array of reference waveform evaluations
h1:        parameterized waveform function 
mynorm:    function s.t. mynorm(f,dt) is a discrete norm


Input expectations
==================
(i) h1 should be defined on a larger temporal grid 
    than t and, hence, h2_ref. Why? When looking for the 
    minimum, h1 will be evaluated at times t + deltaT. 
    t should be viewed as the "common set of times" on which both 
    h1 and h_ref are known.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.create_common_time_grid
documentation: from temporal grids t1 and t2, create grid of common times

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.minimize_waveform_match
documentation: write me: should pass vector of values to h1... will generalize

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.minimize_norm_error
documentation:
Input
===== 
t1,h1:      time/waveform vectors sampled at equally spaced times
tref,href:  a pair of reference time/waveform vectors
mynorm:     norm function (e.g. euclidean_norm_sqrd(f,dx) ) which takes a vector f
t_low,t_up: adjusments  to "clip" the start and end portions of the reference waveform.
            This is useful when the h1 and href durations are very similar in size
            so href need to be restricted a bit to allow the h1 wavefor to "slide" 
            over it.

Output
====== 
guessed:      relative norm error with discrete "guess" for tc and phic offsets 
min_norm:     minimized norm error by solving optimization problem
tc, phic:     time/phase offsets which solve the 2D minimization problem
common_times: Common time grid on which the minimization problem was solved
h1_align:     h1 waveform matched to href
href_eval:    href_eval(common_times) ~ h1_align(common_times)

Output in form [guessed, min_norm]  [tc, phic]  [common_times, h1_align, href_eval]


Input expectations
================== 
(i)  t1 and tref should be equally spaced grid of times. But the dt's need not be the same. 
     For different dt's, the common time grid's dt = min( dt_1, dt_ref)
(ii) for waveforms of different length, (t1,h1) pair should be longer

Output caveats
============== 
(i) evaluating the norm with values of (tc,phic) might give slightly different answers
    depending on the order of shifts/interpolants etc.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.minimize_norm_error_multi_mode
documentation:
performs single-mode match for ell m set in ell_m_match. Uses optimal match parameters 
for multi-mode waveform alignment.

Input
===== 
t1,h1:       time/waveform vectors sampled at equally spaced times. h1 can be an
             array or dictionary of modes
tref,href:   a pair of reference time/waveform vectors. href can be an
             array or dictionary of modes
mode_list:   list of (ell,m) modes that match the ordering of the modes 
             in h1 and href. If h1 and href are dictionaries, this should be 
             set to None
ell_m_match: tuple (ell,m) to use for single mode match. Single mode results
             are used to time and phase shift multi-mode waveform
t_low,t_up:  adjusments  to "clip" the start and end portions of the reference waveform.
             This is useful when the h1 and href durations are very similar in size
             so href need to be restricted a bit to allow the h1 wavefor to "slide" 
             over it.

Output
====== 
WRITE ME


Input expectations
================== 
(i)  t1 and tref should be equally spaced grid of times. But the dt's need not be the same. 
     For different dt's, the common time grid's dt = min( dt_1, dt_ref)
(ii) for waveforms of different length, (t1,h1) pair should be longer

Output caveats
============== 
(i) evaluating the norm with values of (tc,phic) might give slightly different answers
    depending on the order of shifts/interpolants etc.

(ii) If m_match is even; odd-m modes might be off by a sign (-1)^m do to physical rotation of
pi about the z-axis (defined here to parallel to the orbital angular momentum vector). So a final
alignment is done before modes are returned

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.h_sphere_builder
documentation:
Returns a function h(t,theta,phi;z_rot,tc). This function can be evaluated 
for rotations about z-axis and returns either (i) a list of modes or (ii) 
evaluation on sphere at (theta, phi)

 INPUT
 =====
 modes --- array of modes (ell,m)
 hp/hc --- matrix modes. each column is a mode evaluation for (ell[i],m[i]) in modes
 t     --- arrary of times at which modes have been evaluated

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.compute_many_mode_errors
documentation:
input: matrix of modes h, h_ref, mode_list, and norm 
output: dictionary relating mode to relative errors h_{ell,m} compared with href_{ell,m}

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.interpolant_h
documentation:
Compute spline interpolant of input data h at samples t.

s is the spline smoothing parameter

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.interpolate_h

Interpolate input data h at samples tout.

INPUT
=====
tin:  input time grid
h:    input waveform such that h = h(tin); h can be a dicionary
tout: output time grid
deg:  degree of spline

Note: h can be a dictionary of modes

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.spline_f
documentation:
Return a function f_sp such that f_sp(t) returns the spline
evaluation of f

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.orbital_phase_from_hlm
documentation:
Compute orbital phase from GW mode h_{ell,m}.

Input
=====
h2m: numpy array
m:   spherical harmonic index m for mode h_{ell,m}

orbital_phase = -  rac{1}{m} arg(h_{ell,m})

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.orbital_frequency_from_hlm
documentation:
Compute orbital frequency from GW mode h_{ell,m}.

computed as a time derivative of orbital_phase. The derivative
is computed by (i) building a smoothing spline of the phase data
(ii) taking a derivative of the spline.

spline degree(=3) and smoothing factor(=0.001) have been set to reasonable
defaults for SXS waveforms. noisier waveforms should use a larger smoothing 
factor and/or lower degre.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.orbital_phase_frequency_from_hlm
documentation:
Compute orbital phase and frequency from  GW mode h_{\ell,m)}.

The phase is computed from a smoothing spline. Please see the documentation 
of orbital_phase_from_hlm and orbital_frequency_from_hlm for more information.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.energy_flux_from_modes
documentation:
Computes the radiated energy and flux from each mode (E_modes), and 
total energy (E_tot) as described in Eq.(2) of arXiv:1802.04276v2 and E0_over_M (Eq. 3). 
    
We assume equal contributions from m>0 and m<0 modes, 
which is only true for binary systems that orbit in the x-y plane.
    
INPUT
=====
h:  The complex gravitational strain as a dictionary of modes.
    Example: h[(2,2)] is an numpy array
time: A numpy array, times at which the gravitational strain is calculated.
M:    Total mass of the binary at the begining of the evolution. Default: 1.0 
tend: compute up to this final time (in units of M)

RETURNS
=====
E: A dictionary which contains...
  (1) E['total'], the total radiated energy by graviational waves over different modes. This 
      Does not include a PN estimated value E0_over_M for flux at E(0)
  (2) E[(l, m)] the total energy radiated that does include a PN estimated value E0_over_M for flux at E(0)


EXAMPLE
=======
# load model
import gwsurrogate as gws
model = gws.LoadSurrogate("NRHybSur3dq8")

# import routine
from gwtools.gwtools import energy_flux_from_modes

# evaluate model
q=2.0
times, h, dynamics = model(q=q,chiA0=[0,0,0],chiB0=[0,0,0], dt=0.1, units='dimensionless', f_low=.005)

# Compute flux
E_fluxes = energy_flux_from_modes(h=h, time=times, q=q, M=1., tend=100)

# plot flux
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.plot(times, E_fluxes['total'],'black',label='total energy flux')
plt.plot(times, E_fluxes[(2,2)],'blue',label='energy flux from 22 mode only')
plt.plot(times, E_fluxes[(2,-2)],'r--',label='energy flux from 22 mode only')
plt.legend()
       
       
NOTE
====
After this code was written, it was realized that similar code is alredy online: 

       https://github.com/dgerosa/surrkick

Future work: directly compare this code to the one above (see https://github.com/dgerosa/surrkick/blob/master/surrkick/surrkick.py#L283)

Author: Estuti Shukla
Date: 9/10/2021

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.bondi_mass_binary
documentation:
Computes Bondi mass as described in Eq.(4) of arXiv:1802.04276v2. Returns M_t as an array. 

INPUT
=====
E: An dictionary which contains the energy radiated over
all the modes (as well as the total energy) by the gravitational wave.
M: Total mass of the binary at the begining of the evolution. Default: 1.0 

RETURNS
=====
M_t: An array which defines the time-dependent (Bondi) mass of the binary.
M_rem_over_M: A float point number which is the remnant mass or the mass of the post-merger 
             of BH in units of the mass at early times. Shown in eq.5 of arXiv:1802.04276v2. 
              
Author: Estuti Shukla
Date: 9/10/2021

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.aLIGOZeroDetHighPower
documentation: Compute the ZeroDetHighPower PSD on a numpy array f
Convenient interfaces for LALsim functions #

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.EOBNRv2_LAL
documentation: Simplified interface to EOBNRv2 (dominant mode). Dist in megaparsecs
def EOBNRv2_LAL(Mtot,q,dt,fmin,Dist,inclination):
""" Dist in megaparsecs"""
Dist = Dist * 1e6 * PC_SI
M1,M2  = gwtools.Mq_to_m1m2(Mtot,q)
hp, hc = lalsim.SimInspiralChooseTDWaveform(phiRef=0.0,deltaT=dt,m1=M1,m2=M2,s1x=0.0,s1y=0.0,s1z=0.0,\
r=Dist,i=inclination,lambda1=0.0,lambda2=0.0,\
waveFlags=None,nonGRparams=None,amplitudeO=0,phaseO=7,\
approximant=lalsim.EOBNRv2)
h      = hp.data.data + (1j)*hc.data.data
times  = np.arange(np.size(hp.data.data))*hp.deltaT
times,h = gwtools.remove_amplitude_zero(times,h) # removes end portion of waveform which is zero
return times, h

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.generate_LAL_modes
documentation:
Returns a dictionary of modes and array of times.
    
    INPUT
    =====
    approximant -- Model used (ex. lalsim.EOBNRv2)
    q           -- Mass Ratio (M1/M2)
    chiA0       -- Spin of black hole 1 ([S1x,S1y,S1z] dimensionless)
    chiB0       -- Spin of black hole 2 ([S2x,S2y,S2z] dimensionless)
    dt          -- Waveform sampling (s)
    M           -- Mass in Solar Masses
    dist_mpc    -- Distance in MegaParsecs
    f_low       -- Lowest/Starting frequency (Hz)
    f_ref       -- Frequency value, arbitrary (Hz)
    phi_ref     -- Phi at f_ref (radians)


Note: The first version of this routine was written by Vijay Varma
      for the NR surrogate (q=4) code review.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.fft_timeseries
documentation:
Take an FFT of LAL datatype REAL8 or COMPLEX16 TimeSeries

 This routine was originally written by Evan Ochsner for lalsimwaves.py

INPUT
=====
h -- time-series (e.g. strain hp*Fp + hc+Fc). h must be a REAL8TimeSeries
     which, for example, results from add hp and hc as returned 
     from  hp, hc = lalsim.SimInspiralChooseTDWaveform(...
df     -- Setting df=0 means the TD waveform will just pads to the next power of 2.
          For df!=0, the TD waveform will be padded further, or it will print a
          warning if the waveform is so long that it requires a smaller df.

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.REAL8_TAPER_from_numpy
documentation:
From real timeseries h and deltaT dt, create a REAL8TimeSeries
object and taper. 

INPUT
=====
h           --- a real-valued numpy array representing the timeseries
dt          --- time interval between samples
sampleUnits --- lal.XXX units tag (e.g. lal.SecondUnit)
taper       --- lalsim.XXX taper tag (e.g. lalsim.SIM_INSPIRAL_TAPER_STARTEND)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.COMPLEX16_TAPER_from_numpy
documentation:
From complex timeseries h and deltaT dt, create a Complex16TimeSeries
object and taper. 

INPUT
=====
h           --- a complex-valued numpy array representing the timeseries
dt          --- time interval between samples
sampleUnits --- lal.XXX units tag (e.g. lal.SecondUnit)
taper       --- lalsim.XXX taper tag (e.g. lalsim.SIM_INSPIRAL_TAPER_STARTEND)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.fft_from_numpy
documentation:
From real or complex timeseries, h, and deltaT, dt, taper and
perform an FFT using LAL code.

INPUT
=====
h           --- a real or complex valued numpy array representing the timeseries
dt          --- time interval between samples
sampleUnits --- lal.XXX units tag (e.g. lal.SecondUnit)
taper       --- lalsim.XXX taper tag (e.g. lalsim.SIM_INSPIRAL_TAPER_STARTEND).
                To use a non-LAL taper, taper before passing h to this routine
                and set taper = lalsim.SIM_INSPIRAL_TAPER_NONE

OUTPUT
======
hf --- FFT as a LAL timeseries (COMPLEX16FrequencySeries)
TODO: should have set of routines which use numpy fft.fft (see Jonathan's code)

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.modes_dict_to_list
documentation:
Convert a dictionary of modes into a list of modes whose elements 
are numpy vectors (the time series). 

If an array of modes is given, the returned mode_list list will have exactly 
that ordering.
Functions for changing data representation
of multi-mode waveforms dictionary <-> list

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.modes_list_to_dict
documentation:
Convert a numpy array indexed by list of modes into a dictionary
of modes. 

Assumes elements of mode_list can be used as a dictionary hash.

   Ex: mode_list = [(2,2), (3,3)]
       h[0,:]  -> 22 mode
       h[1,:]  -> 33 mode

functions.name: gwtools.gwtools.sxs_memory
documentation:
Compute memory modes using sxs module. Make sure sxs is installed as

  >>> conda install -c conda-forge sxs

Input
=====
h_lm:    dictionary of oscillatory modes. For example,

             hdict_tmp[(2,2)] = np.array( [...] )

times:   array of times defining the hdict_tmp modes
verbose: Output verbosity
mem_additional_ell: compute memory modes beyond ell_max found from h_lm

   Example: if the largest ell in h_lm is ell=3, and mem_additional_ell=2
            then compute memory modes up to ell=5.

   Note: compute time quickly goes up with ell!

integration_start_time: float, optional
      Time at which the energy flux integral should begin.  The default is None (times[0] used)


Output
======
memory_sxs: dictionary of memory modes
### convenient wrapper to sxs memory interface ###

variables.name: gwtools.gwtools.PC_SI
documentation: Parsec in meters
